data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Warren Buffett Is Teaching Us A Lesson (Downgrade To Hold)
data.description:
After a tumultuous four weeks for the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY), I was really looking forward to none other than Warren Buffett’s annual letter that typically affords us helpful and constructive (life and investing) insights, while setting the stage for Berkshire Hathaway’s annual meeting (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) later in May. With Berkshire stock having outperformed the Magnificent Seven dominated S&P 500, I think it’s an opportune moment for us to take a step back and consider the wisdom encapsulated in Buffett's missive, as SPX has failed to break decisively above the 6,100 levels since early December.

Investors have already been given a preamble into Buffett’s current thinking, as we assess the recent significant trades for Berkshire in Q4. While Buffett’s lack of repurchasing Berkshire stock in Q4 arguably highlights his assessment of elevated market valuations, he also made clear that Berkshire will maintain its preference in the allocation of capital into equities instead of cash and their equivalents, which I believe validates Buffett’s belief in the underlying long-term strength of the American equities market, downplaying the bearish prognisticators who think that we could be at the “cusp” of a long term bear market. As a reminder, Buffett highlighted:

Berkshire shareholders can rest assured that we will forever deploy a substantial majority of their money in equities – mostly American equities although many of these will have international operations of significance. Berkshire will never prefer ownership of cash-equivalent assets over the ownership of good businesses, whether controlled or only partially owned. - (Warren Buffett annual letter 2024)

I emphasized in my previous Berkshire article that investors must be careful about being struck with “analysis paralysis” into Buffett’s decision to sell more stock. I added that the reallocation suggests an astute move to lower concentration risks, which in itself is a prudent capital allocation strategy, and not immediately indicative of a bearish signal. Therefore, I believe Buffett’s assurance in his annual letter about Berkshire’s capital deployment demonstrates his conviction in an equity-heavy strategy, even though the lack of “bargains” has likely driven his decision to not buy back Berkshire stock in Q4, which is again a prudent move. It’s critical for investors not to misconstrue Buffett’s signal as bearish, and there couldn’t have been a clearer message from the Berkshire Chairman and CEO into staying invested in high quality, and well managed companies.

Berkshire’s decision to shed significant holdings of Apple Inc. (AAPL) and Bank of America (BAC) has likely led to substantial capital gains taxes in 2024, although they are still within the conglomerate’s top ten holdings, underlining Buffett’s conviction about their long-term potential. Hence, I urge investors to ignore the fallacy of buying into certain media bias that may have flagged his reduction in these companies as suggestive of red flags in his assessment of the market.

Also, Buffett has helped build up a substantial cash pile of $334B as of Q4, even though as it reduced the portfolio exposure of its publicly listed portfolio, which is currently valued at just over $295B. Hence, I believe Berkshire has a massive stockpile of ammo ready to deploy if needed to capitalize on steep market selloffs, proffering investors' confidence in the conglomerate’s ability to drive substantial shareholder value when the opportunity arises.

As a result, I believe it should not only help keep investors on board, but also drive higher conviction in value investors knowing that Buffett is careful and judicious in capital allocation decisions. And his overwhelming confidence in equities suggests Berkshire expects to continue spurring growth through its public and non-public holdings, reinvesting its profits, without the need to pander to the needs of income investors (since Berkshire doesn’t pay a dividend).

In addition, I think investors shouldn’t forget that Berkshire has also astutely hedged its marketable equity exposure with controlled businesses that generate substantial operating earnings. Insurance underwriting remains a core business for Berkshire, not just for its contribution to operating profits, but also the massive $171B in float that offers Berkshire a “very cheap” source of funds to generate investment income that has surged more than 48% year-on-year. Therefore, even as the growth in operating earnings for its controlled (main and other) and non-controlled businesses could slow, the demonstrated scale and underwriting capabilities in Berkshire’s insurance business will remain a significant competitive advantage.

However, recent climate catastrophes as seen in the recent wildfires have also injected new “unknown” algorithms into insurance underwriting for Berkshire’s P&C division, and even Buffett acknowledged that the long-term tail risks could be massive, as he emphasized:

Climate change may have been announcing its arrival. However, no “monster” event occurred during 2024. Someday, any day, a truly staggering insurance loss will occur – and there is no guarantee that there will be only one per annum. - (Buffett annual letter)

The silver lining underscoring Berkshire’s ability to manage risks well is its inherent posture toward risk management (i.e. aversion, but not paralysis), and its corroborated capabilities to deploy capital well to generate substantial investment returns over time. Coupled with its scale (to manage risks and pricing appropriately) and float, Buffett recognizes that it isn’t something that any insurer can replicate easily, validating the sustainable moat that has been painstakingly developed and strengthened over time, as he articulated:

No private insurer has the willingness to take on the amount of risk that Berkshire can provide. At times, this advantage can be important. But we also need to shrink when prices are inadequate. We must never write inadequately-priced policies in order to stay in the game. - (Buffett annual letter)

Still, Berkshire stock is now valued at just over 24.4x operating earnings on a forward basis, a premium of less than 10% over its five-year average. It also trades at a premium over the S&P 500’s 22x forward earnings multiple, which could suggest some form of caution is necessary. Not just that, the lack of stock buybacks in Q4 is indicative of Buffett’s belief that even its stock may not be that attractive now when considering its valuation. Compounded with the possibly uncertain leadership transition risks to Vice Chair Greg Abel (although he has Buffett’s full confidence), I believe there are sufficient reasons for us to be careful in thinking about adding Berkshire stock aggressively here.

Furthermore, I think it’s increasingly clear that BRK.B stock hasn’t managed to overcome the $485 resistance zone since September last year, although support is assessed to be robust just over the $435 level. In other words, while no bearish signals is ascertained in Berkshire stock’s price chart, I believe the lack of follow on momentum over the past six months suggest we should buy closer to the $435 level, while avoiding adding until it manages to break above the $485 level and establish a new consolidation zone.

Berkshire remains a fundamentally strong stock and Buffett’s assurance of keeping his faith in equities should assure us that any talk about approaching a structural bearish turning point in the market is premature and possibly overstated. Yet, caution about adding more BRK.B now seems to be apt, as evinced by Buffett’s reticence in buying back more Berkshire stock in Q4. Hence, a Hold rating for now is likely the right move, while investors await another more attractive opportunity to add more positions.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

data.title: Why SkyWest Is A Strong Buy: Undervalued Stock With Growing Profitability
data.description:
SkyWest (NASDAQ:SKYW) looks like a seriously undervalued stock with big upside potential. The market is still stuck on old worries about pilot shortages and airline struggles, but that’s missing the bigger picture. SkyWest isn’t like other airlines—it makes most of its money from fixed-fee contracts with major carriers like United (UAL), Delta (DAL), American (AAL) and Alaska (ALK), meaning it doesn’t rely on ticket prices or fuel costs as much as other airlines.

The company is really performing well right now. Its net income jumped from $34.3M in 2023 to $322M in 2024. Operating income for 2024 hit $494.7M, up 375% from last year, and gross profit jumped 39% YoY to $2.17B. That’s not the kind of company that should be trading at a cheap 11.3x forward P/E, especially when its five-year average is 46.9x.

With $801.6M in liquidity, rising free cash flow and an improving balance sheet, SkyWest is in much better financial shape than most investors realize. The stock is trading way below its real value and I think it should be worth at least $140 which is 37% higher than where it is now.

Why The Market Is Getting It Wrong

Investors are still treating SkyWest like just another airline, but that’s not really the case. Most airlines make money selling tickets, so when the economy slows down, their profits take a hit. SkyWest, on the other hand, gets paid no matter what because of its long-term contracts with the big airlines. Even if fewer people fly, SkyWest still gets its check. That stability is something the market hasn’t really priced in yet.

Then there’s the pilot shortage narrative, which is another reason some investors have stayed away. It’s true that in 2022 and early 2023 SkyWest struggled to keep pilots and that made it hard to fly all its scheduled routes. But that problem has gotten a lot better. The company raised pay, brought in more pilots, and flight volume is already back on the rise. In 2024 departures were up 10.8% and block hours increased 13.3% year-over-year, meaning they’re flying more and making more money again.

Another thing people aren’t seeing is how smart SkyWest has been with its fleet. Instead of just adding planes for the sake of it, the company has upgraded to more fuel-efficient models like the Embraer E175 and CRJ900. That means lower maintenance costs and better margins which is one of the reasons EBITDA margins jumped to 24.8% crushing the industry average of 14.4%.

SkyWest’s Financials Are Way Stronger Than People Think

If you look at SkyWest’s numbers, it’s pretty clear that this is a company on the upswing.

EBITDA is soaring, rising from $554M in 2023 to $930M in 2024. EBIT margins hit 13.9% much better than the sector median of 10.4% showing that management is running a much tighter operation.

Debt has been a concern for some investors, but that’s also improving fast. The company’s Debt/EBITDA ratio has gone from 7.2x in 2022 to just 4.8x in 2024, which means debt is becoming much more manageable. On top of that, free cash flow is exploding—SkyWest generated $216.5M in FCF in Q2 2024, up 60% from the year before. That kind of cash flow is exactly what investors should want to see, but the stock price doesn’t reflect it yet.

The other thing to note is CapEx (capital spending) is starting to slow down which means more cash is staying in the business instead of being spent on new planes. This puts SkyWest in a great spot to keep improving profits while also paying down debt.

The Stock Is Too Cheap Compared to Its Peers

At 11.3x forward P/E SkyWest is trading way below airline industry averages. Its EV/EBITDA ratio is just 6.44x, well below the industry median of 11.92x which just doesn’t make sense given its strong recovery.

Here’s what that means in dollar terms. If SkyWest keeps growing earnings like it has been, I think EPS could hit $10.00 by 2025. At a reasonable 14x P/E multiple, the stock should be worth $140. Even in a base case where EPS comes in at $8.50, a 12x multiple would put the stock at $102 right around where it’s trading now. The only way I see this stock dropping much lower is in a worst-case scenario where EPS falls to $6.00 and the P/E drops to 9x multiple. That would put it at $54, which is a big drop, but it would take something serious—like a major economic slowdown or a big hit to SkyWest’s business to get there.

That’s why I think this setup is so good. The stock is already cheap compared to its competitors and if earnings keep improving, there’s real potential for a 30-40% gain, while the probability of a major collapse is much lower.

What’s the Market Saying?

The news on SkyWest has been mostly positive, but investors aren’t fully reacting to it yet. The overall news sentiment is somewhat bullish and short interest in the stock has dropped by 7.17% meaning fewer traders are betting against it.

One red flag that some people point to is that CFO Robert Simmons recently sold $681K worth of stock. While that might seem like a negative, it’s actually a pretty small amount compared to the company’s $4.1B market cap. More importantly, institutional investors are holding steady, which means the people who manage big money aren’t worried.

What Could Go Wrong?

SkyWest looks like a solid investment, but every stock has risks. The key is understanding what could go wrong and deciding if those risks are deal-breakers or just bumps in the road. Here’s what I think investors should keep in mind.

Pilot Shortages and Keeping Talent

SkyWest, like every other regional airline, has had a rough time with pilot shortages over the last few years. The FAA made pilot training rules stricter, and many older pilots retired early. Big airlines have also been poaching regional pilots by offering better pay and perks, which makes it harder for SkyWest to keep its staff.

They’ve taken steps to fix this by raising pay and improving benefits, and it’s already helped. In 2024 flights and block hours were up compared to last year, which means they’re flying more. But if another wave of pilots leaves or if hiring slows down again, it could put a cap on their growth.

Relying on Big Airline Partners

Unlike Delta or United, SkyWest doesn’t sell its own tickets. It flies under contracts with the big airlines, and they pay SkyWest a set fee per flight. This setup is great for stable cash flow, but it also means SkyWest has no control over its own destiny. If one of its major airline partners decides to cut back on regional flying or switch to another regional airline, SkyWest could be in trouble.

Right now, they’ve got solid long-term contracts in place, so it’s not an issue in the short term. But if an airline like United decides to fly more of its own regional routes instead of outsourcing to SkyWest, that would be a major hit. It’s something to keep an eye on.

Fuel Prices and Prorate Agreements

Most of the time, SkyWest doesn’t have to worry about fuel prices because its airline partners cover those costs under fixed-fee agreements. But not all of SkyWest’s flying is protected—they also have prorate agreements where they share revenue with airlines but have to pay for fuel themselves.

If fuel prices shoot up, those prorate flights could become a lot less profitable. Right now, about 13% of SkyWest’s revenue comes from these agreements, so it’s not a dealbreaker, but it’s definitely something that could hurt margins if oil prices spike unexpectedly.

Stock Price Swings and Market Volatility

The airline industry is one of the most volatile sectors and stocks can swing wildly based on things like fuel prices, travel demand or economic conditions.

Even if SkyWest is doing everything right, if the market turns against airline stocks, SKYW could still take a hit. Investors need to be prepared for some big ups and downs along the way.

SkyWest isn’t risk-free, but most of these risks are manageable. The pilot shortage problem is improving, airline contracts are locked in for now, and the company has a strong balance sheet to weather bumps in the road.

The biggest risks to watch are pilot retention, airline contract renewals and potential fuel price spikes. None of these are dealbreakers, but they could cause some short-term turbulence for the stock.

For long-term investors, these risks seem worth taking given the upside potential. SkyWest isn’t just another struggling airline—it’s a well-run business with strong cash flow and an improving outlook. If the market finally catches on, there’s a lot of room for this stock to climb.

Final Verdict: A Strong Buy With Big Upside

SkyWest is one of the best risk-reward setups in the airline industry right now. The market hasn’t fully priced in its profit rebound, improving financials and lower debt risk. With earnings growing fast, margins expanding and cash flow getting stronger, this is a stock that should be worth a lot more than what it’s trading for today.

At $102 per share, SkyWest is still too cheap. Based on what I see this should be a $140 stock within the next 12-18 months which is a 37% gain from here. Given the low valuation, strong financials and reduced risks, I think this is an easy Strong Buy. If the market doesn’t see it now, it will soon enough.

data.title: Bayer: A Contrarian Thesis
data.description:
Bayer AG (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) operates in three segments: Crop Sciences, Pharmaceuticals and Consumer Health.

Pharmaceutical Division

Bayer’s pharmaceutical division generates ~€18 billion annually with 28.7% EBITDA margins. The division’s second best-selling drug (Xarelto) will face increasing generic competition in Canada and Europe in 2025 and is expected to continue its decline. However, the division’s revenue has remained remarkably stable due to growth in other best-selling drugs, such as Elyea, and high double-digit growth in relatively recently commercialized drugs (such as Nubeqa and Karendia). In fact, the pharmaceutical division revenue grew 4% last quarter in spite of such LoE concerns.

In addition to a diversified commercialized portfolio, the pharmaceutical division’s late-stage pipeline is well-developed. While its renewed product mix is anticipated to negatively impact margins, sales should, at least, remain stable.

Crop Sciences

Its Crop Sciences division, formerly Monsanto, was acquired for $63 billion in June of 2018, more than Bayer’s current enterprise value. Bayer took on an initial $57 billion in debt to finance the transaction. Since the acquisition, Bayer’s Crop Sciences division has been marred by litigation, primarily over its glyphosate-based herbicide Roundup.

Its Crop Sciences R&D pipeline is less critical, however, as the loss of exclusivity(LoE)/patent problems does not plague the crop sciences industry to the extent it does the pharmaceutical industry. Nevertheless, its crop sciences division has 13 products in its crop protection pipeline alone. The division’s seed, trait and digital product R&D pipelines are similarly expansive. While the majority of the aforementioned pipelines are upgrades to existing agribusiness technology, designed to stabilize revenue, some, such as the Precion Smart Corn System, are due for commercialization within the next few years and may drive unanticipated sales growth.

Bayer’s Crop Science’s division is its largest and most profitable. It also appears to contain the most upside, given its expansive R&D pipeline. While amongst the most dominant players in agribusiness technology, its growth potential is limited, but significant. Its R&D pipeline is designed to both stabilize revenue from existing products, and generate differentiation (and therefore pricing power) by the introduction of new products. It appears the market is assigning an improperly low value to Bayer’s crop science division in spite of the ongoing Glyphosate litigation.

Consumer Health

Bayer’s consumer health division is defined by products such as Aspirin and Claritin. These consumer brands’ financial performance are typified by stability, and sales growth largely matches market growth. Bayer plans to accelerate EBITDA growth through its efficiency measures (new operating model) and increasing the speed at which it can deliver new products to market to accelerate “customer centricity.” While unlikely to outperform market growth in the top-line, its efficiency measures may well lead to margin expansion.

Pharmaceutical R&D Pipeline

In spite of its promising late-stage pipeline, with two drugs (Elinzanent and Acorimidis) rapidly approaching commercialization, the company’s early-stage pipeline is less promising. Management has repeatedly announced its intention to “rapidly rebuild [it’s] early-stage pipeline.” While management’s constrained optionality is prohibitive for much early-stage M&A, when Bayer reaches its target leverage ratio it may begin to explore inorganic pipeline growth again.

Glyphosate Litigation

Perhaps the most pressing concern for Bayer surrounds ongoing litigation about its glyphosate-based herbicide Roundup. To date, Bayer has paid over €15 billion in claims to settle over 100,000 lawsuits relating to its products. However, Bayer’s legal settlements have only resulted in an €889 million loss in the last twelve months. Although the company believes that the “worst is behind us,” more than 50,000 lawsuits remain in court. Bayer has won many of these lawsuits and believes that the “science is on our side.”

The worst of the litigation payouts occurred in 2020, when Bayer paid out €13.3 billion in legal settlements. Legal settlements have moderated since, and have not surpassed €1 billion since 2021. It appears the market is overreacting to the glyphosate litigation when viewed relatively.

New Operating model

The company’s new CEO, Bill Anderson, has promised to effectuate cost savings through “reduc[ing] hierarchy levels, eliminat[ing] bureaucracy and significantly accelerat[ing] decisionmaking.” Under the moniker of ‘Dynamic Shared Ownership,’ management estimates this will result in a “considerable number of job reductions,” therefore reducing operating expenditures. It expects such ongoing efficiency measures to “partially offset” lower margins in its pharmaceutical division. Consensus estimates still project EBITDA margins to fall by 1.9% in 2025. This is in-line with management’s tempered expectations of its cost-cutting measures, and any additional margin expansion will likely be viewed favorably by the market.

Capital Allocation

In Q1 of 2024, Bayer cut its dividend from €2.40 to €0.11 to “redu[ce] debt and increa[se] flexibility.” Bayer’s current net debt stands at €34.8 billion. Using consensus estimates, this results in a leverage ratio of approximately 3.5x. While still high, and in spite of Bayer’s intention to lower its leverage ratio to 2.5x, it does not preclude Bayer from engaging in M&A to redevelop its mid, and particularly early-stage, pharmaceutical R&D pipeline. While its arguably overleveraged capital structure may constrain Bayer’s optionality, it lessens the degree of potential misallocation through inopportune or excessive inorganic growth. Capital is unlikely to be returned to shareholders in the near-term, but debt reduction alone, excluding the possibility of EBITDA growth, will allow Bayer to reach its target leverage ratio in the medium term. Once the target leverage ratio is reached, Bayer may well resume capital returns to shareholders or return to inorganic pipeline development.

Valuation

Bayer’s EV/EBITDA multiple stands at approximately 5.5x. While undemanding, this does not alleviate concerns with Bayer’s leverage ratio. Nonetheless, it provides an ample margin of safety for equity investors. A 5-year DCF, using a 10% discount rate, consensus estimates through 2027, assuming 2027’s consensus 1.27% revenue growth and 2027 margins through 2029, and a terminal growth rate of 2% will yield an upside of ~34%, or almost the exact equivalent of the street target price of €27.51 ($28.59), as of February 14.

Relative Valuation

Bayer is difficult to value on a relative basis due to the combination of its three divisions. I will be performing a simplistic relative valuation on each of its divisions based upon it and its competitors EV/EBITDA multiples. For Bayer’s crop science division I selected two chemical companies with a presence in agribusiness: DuPont and BASF. I selected Merck and Johnson & Johnson for the pharmaceutical relative valuation as both companies have diversified commercialized product portfolios that are not distorted by GLP-1 agonists. For Bayer’s consumer health division I again selected Johnson & Johnson and Bausch & Lomb. As one can see, Bayer is undervalued relative to its peers in all three segments. Bayer’s relative undervaluation, according to this analysis, is greater than its absolute undervaluation. While this relative valuation was by no means exhaustive, that six of its peers in three segments trade at a significant premium nonetheless demonstrates that there may be greater upside for the stock than an isolated absolute valuation would suggest.

Conclusion

Management seems credible in its claim that the worst of the glyphosate litigation is over. The LoE concerns surrounding its pharmaceutical division’s Xarelto will be offset by the growth of its recently commercialized Nubeqa and Karendia. Additionally, Bayer’s expansive late-stage pipeline includes drugs, such as Elinzanetant and Acorimidis, that are likely to be commercialized in 2025. Such products are sure to at least stabilize the pharmaceutical division's revenues. Furthermore, any moderate margin compression that will occur from a less favorable product mix in Bayer’s pharmaceutical division will likely be dampened by Bayer’s new operating model.

That Bayer’s high leverage ratio will constrain its capital allocation optionality seems likely, but not certain. Bayer may well engage in advantageous M&A to expand its early and mid-stage pipeline. Furthermore, management’s ‘Dynamic Shared Ownership’ may yield additional, unaccounted for upside for shareholders. Bayer’s glyphosate litigation is unlikely to worsen, but will likely result in significant, but anticipated, settlements in the next few years. The size of such settlements are nearly inestimable, but their trend is positive (for Bayer).

When Bayer’s current market value is appraised, it appears that its risk profile is favorably asymmetric; its challenges are more than adequately priced and the possibility of outperformance is discounted.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 5 Best CEFs This Month: Average Yield Of 10% (February 2025)
data.description:
Author's Note: This article is part of our monthly series that tries to discover the five best buys in the CEF arena at that point in time. Certain parts of the introduction, definitions, and sections describing selection criteria/process may have some commonality and repetitiveness with our other articles in this series. This is unavoidable and intentional to keep the entire series consistent and easy to follow for new readers. Such sections or texts will generally appear in "italics." Regular readers who follow the series from month to month could skip the general introduction and sections describing the selection process (please skip to "Narrowing Down To 50 Funds"). Also, those who need a detailed introduction to our selection process can read our blog post here.

Please note that these are not recommendations to buy, but should be considered as a starting point for further research.

Introduction:

The market has gained a lot of ground in the last couple of months. However, there has been a bit of pause recently due to uncertainty about the Fed's path this year, but by most estimates, the momentum is still positive. As a result, the market is expensive, and so are most CEFs (closed-end funds) to some extent. It is getting harder to find attractive funds at good discounts. That said, some sectors that have struggled recently due to a slower pace of rate decline are still appealing.

Why CEFs?

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal level of yield of less than 1.25%. Income investors and retirees can't survive on that level of income unless they have a very large pool of capital. Alternatively, one can find solid blue chip individual dividend stocks that pay much higher than the S&P500. They can also provide market-matching growth on a longer-term basis. To identify such stocks, we also publish a monthly article on the '5 Best DGI stocks'. You can find the most recent one here.

However, with CEFs, we can take the income to the next level. That said, they generally come with higher risks (discussed later), so we believe that an allocation of 20% to 30% to a diversified CEF portfolio should be good enough for most income investors.

With the interest rates set to decline (even if at a slower pace), the CEFs generally performed very well in 2024, with some sectors better than others. Even though some of the outperformance is behind us, the positive momentum has continued in 2025. That said, we like to invest for the long term, and these investments are primarily meant for income.

We publish this monthly article on CEFs (Closed-end funds) to provide our readers with a select list of 5 to 10 CEFs that, as a group, provide wide diversification in terms of underlying assets, solid long-term performance at least on a relative basis, and are trading at decent valuations. Above all, the income goal for the group is to provide a yield that is at least 8% or higher.

This month, as a group, the selected funds are offering an average distribution rate of well over 10% (as of 02/14/2025). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had a decent record over the long term and collectively returned 11.26% and 9.93% in the last five and ten years, annualized. The 3-year average return is reasonable, too, at 8.6%. The average discount (to NAV) is decent, at nearly -6.8%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Why Is It Important To Have A Diversified CEF Portfolio?

We believe a well-diversified CEF portfolio should contain at least 10 CEFs, preferably from different asset classes. It's also advisable to build the portfolio over a period rather than invest in one lump sum. If you were to invest in one CEF every month for a year, you would have a well-diversified CEF portfolio by the year's end. What we provide here every month is a list of five probable candidates for further research. A CEF portfolio can be an important component of the overall portfolio strategy. One should preferably have a DGI portfolio as the foundation, and the CEF portfolio could be used to boost the income level to the desired level. How much should one allocate to CEFs? Each investor needs to answer this question himself/herself based on their personal situation and factors like the size of the portfolio, income needs, risk appetite, or risk tolerance.

Criteria to Shortlist:

Criteria

Brings down the number of funds to...

Reason for the Criteria

Baseline expense < 2.5% and Avg. Daily Volume > 10,000

Approx. 435 Funds

We do not want funds that charge excessive fees. Also, we want funds that have fair liquidity.

Market capitalization> 100 Million

Approx. 400 Funds

We do not want funds that are too small.

Track record/ History longer than five years (inception date 2016 or earlier)

Approx. 375 Funds

We want funds that have a reasonably long track record.

Discount/Premium < +7%

Approx. 350 Funds

We do not want to pay too high a premium; we want bigger discounts.

Distribution (dividend) Rate > 5%

Approx. 250-290 Funds

The current distribution (income) is reasonably high.

5-Year Annualized Return on NAV > 0% AND/ OR

3-Year Annualized Return on NAV >0%

Approx. 200-250 Funds

We want funds with a reasonably good past track record in maintaining their NAVs.

After we applied the above criteria this month, we are generally left with around 250 funds (plus or minus 5%) on our list. From here on, we apply our other criteria to narrow the list.

Note: All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article are sourced from Cefconnect.com, Cefa.com, and Morningstar.com.

Narrowing Down To 50 Funds

To narrow down the number of funds to a more manageable count, we will shortlist ten funds based on each of the following criteria. Thereafter, we will apply certain qualitative criteria to each fund and rank them to select the top five.

At this stage, we also eliminate certain funds with substantially negative NAV returns for both three-year and five-year periods.

Seven Broad Criteria:

  • Excess discount/premium (explained below).
  • Distribution rate.
  • Return on NAV, last three years (medium-term).
  • Return on NAV, last five years (long term).
  • Coverage ratio.
  • Excess return over distributions.
  • The total weight (calculated up to this point).

Excess Discount/Premium:

We certainly like funds that are offering large discounts (not premiums) to their NAVs. But what's important is to look at the "excess discount/premium" and not the absolute value. We want to see the discount (or premium) relative to their past record, say 52-week average.

Excess Discount/Premium = Current Discount/Premium (Minus) 52-Wk Avg. Discount/ Premium.

For more explanation of the excess discount and z-scores, please refer to our blog post (link provided at the beginning of the article).

We sort our list (of approx. 250 funds) on the "excess discount/premium" in descending order. For this criterion, the lower the value, the better it is. So, we select the top 10 funds (most negative values) from this sorted list.

(All data as of 02/14/2024)

Table 1:

High Current Distribution Rate:

After all, most investors invest in CEF funds for their juicy distributions. Even though we should not chase yield, at this stage, we want to give them a fair representation in our list and subject them to a more rigorous criterion later on. We sorted our list on the current distribution rate (descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds from this sorted list.

Table 2:

Medium-Term Return on NAV (last three years):

We then sort our list on a three-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and select the top 10 funds. Please note that the huge drawdowns in the case of MLPs during the pandemic (caused by the Oil crash) and subsequent recovery have led to too many MLPs and energy funds topping this list (3-year NAV gains). For this reason, we make a shortlist based on the total score and keep only a few of them on this list.

Table 3:

Five-Year Annualized Return on NAV:

We then sorted our list on the five-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 4:

Coverage Ratio (Distributions Vs. Earnings):

The coverage ratio is derived by dividing the earnings per share by the distribution amount for a specific period. Please note that in some cases, the coverage ratio may be a bit inaccurate since the "earnings per share" may be three to six months old. But in most cases, it's fairly accurate. Furthermore, the coverage ratio is not critical for pure equity funds as they generate very little dividend income, and they cover most of their distributions from capital gains. However, we sorted our list on the coverage ratio and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 5:

Excess Return Over Distribution:

If a fund provides a very high distribution but low returns over 3, 5, or 10-year periods, it does not help the long-term interests of the investor. In simple terms, the fund is just overpaying the distributions and hurting the NAV in the long term. That is why we calculate a factor we like to call "excess return." This excess return is what is provided by the fund over and above the distribution rate over a specified time period. We calculate it by subtracting the distribution rate from the average of three-year and five-year NAV returns. In this list, we included the top 10 funds.

Table 6:

Total Weight (Quality Score) Calculated Up to This Point:

Note: The Total Weight calculation is not fully completed at this point, since we have not considered the 10-year NAV return. However, we select the top 15 names based on current calculations.

Table 7:

From the selections so far, we have 75 funds in total (tables 1-7).

We will need to see if there are any duplicates among them. In our current list of 75 funds, there were 25 duplicates, meaning there are funds that appeared more than once. The following names appear twice (or more):

Appeared twice: ADX, CCIF, CSQ, EMO, HGLB, KIO, KYN, SCD, TWN, XFLT (10 duplicates).

Appeared three times: ETY, PEO, STK (6 duplicates).

Appeared four times: CET, NML, PDX (9 duplicates).

Once we remove 25 duplicate rows, we are left with 50 (75 - 25) funds.

Note: It may be worthwhile to mention here that just because a fund has appeared multiple times does not necessarily make it an attractive candidate. Sometimes, a fund may appear multiple times simply for the wrong reasons, like a high current discount, high excess discount, or a very high distribution rate that may not be sustainable. However, during the second filtering stage, it may not score well on the overall quality score due to other factors like poor track record. That said, if a fund has appeared four times or more, it may be worth a second look.

Narrowing Down to Just 10 Funds

In our list of funds, we already may have some of the best probable candidates. However, so far, they have been selected based on one (or two) criteria that each may be good at. That's not nearly enough. So, we will apply a combination of criteria by applying weights to eight factors to calculate the total quality score and filter out the best ones.

We will apply weights to each of the eight criteria:

Criteria

Max. Weight

Comment

Baseline expense

Excess discount/premium

5-YR NAV return

10-YR NAV return or 10-YR Total return

If less than ten years of history, an average of three and five-year returns is taken.

Excess NAV return over distribution rate

Adjusted Coverage Ratio

This factor is adjusted for certain factors. The adjustment is performed based on the type of fund, like equity and sector funds.

Once we have calculated the weights, we combine them to calculate the "Total Combined Weight," also called the "Quality Score."

The sorted list (spreadsheet) of 50 funds on the "combined total weight or quality score" is attached here.

File-for-export_-_5_Best_CEFs_-_Feb_25.xlsx

10-Positions Portfolio of The Month

Before we get to the final five, we will narrow down our list from 50 names to 20. So, we sorted our table based on the sector and then the quality score. Furthermore, in this round of selections, we mostly pick one or two top funds from each sector (based on quality score). There is a bit of subjective judgment, as we tend to prioritize funds that pay regular and consistent distributions on a monthly or quarterly basis. We also weed out any fund that may have a very poor track record in terms of NAV performance. Funds that may have inconsistent dividends (even if they are high) generally do not make it to our top list. Furthermore, be aware that many times, single-country funds score high in our rankings, but most of them pay variable dividends on an annual or semi-annual basis. In addition, being single-country funds, they can be inherently riskier since their future returns are tied to just one country, be it economic, regulatory, or geopolitical factors. For these reasons, they generally do not make it to our top 5 lists, but they do appear on our list of 20. Individual investors could consider them based on their goals.

Here's the list of the top 20 selections (from different asset classes):

Table 8:

From here on, we will have to perform some subjective judgment and narrow the list to 10 names. Here's the list of the top 10 selections this month (in order of sector):

(IIF), (CHY), (QQQX), (PDX), (KIO), (PEO), (BANX), (HQL), (XFLT), and (ADX).

Table-8B:

Final Selection: Our List Of Final Top 5

5-Positions Portfolio of the Month:

So far, we have followed our filtering criteria to a large extent. However, in the end, if we only want to select five funds, we will need to apply our subjective judgment. As such, for new CEF investors, our list of top 10 selections (table-8B) is quite compelling and makes a more diversified list. While we narrow down this list to five, we should be mindful to keep the list as diversified as possible in terms of asset classes. Since this step is mostly subjective, the choice would differ from person to person. Nonetheless, here are the selections for this month, based on our perspective (in order of Quality Score):

Current month selections:

  • Adams Diversified Equity Fund
  • Adams Natural Resources Fund
  • Nuveen NASDAQ 100 Dynamic Overwrite Fund
  • KKR Income Opportunities Fund
  • XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust.

Table: The Final 5 Funds:

Table 9:

Some information about the selections:

We recommend that readers also look at the top-10 list. The top-10 list offers much more of a diversified lot compared to the top 5.

  • Adams Diversified Equity Fund

Every month, we try to select one fund from the US-focused, large-cap core dividend category. Last month, we selected the Central Securities (CET), which continues to be attractive. However, this month, we are highlighting ADX for its better yield and diversification.

Please note that Adams Group changed its distribution policy for ADX in 2024 and committed to distribute at an 8% annualized rate (or 2% on a quarterly basis) of the fund's average net asset value ("NAV"). Currently, ADX yields 9% and offers roughly -10% discount. ADX fund is internally managed, uses no leverage, and charges much lower fees than its peers. Its core philosophy is to invest in a broadly diversified, sector-neutral, large-cap equity portfolio. The fund has a very long and excellent performance history. In the past 3, 5, and 10-year periods, it has returned on the NAV basis 11.05%, 14.40%, and 12.90%, respectively, on an annualized basis.

Some of the top holdings of the fund are similar to that of the S&P500, with many of the Magnificent Seven among them, such as Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta (META), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Broadcom (AVGO), and Eli Lilly (LLY).

  • Adams Natural Resources Fund

PEO has appeared in our top-5 many times in the past. This fund continues to be an attractive candidate with a solid yield of close to 9.50%. However, this is a sector-specific fund, and one should invest in it with that knowledge. If you would like to invest in some of the biggest names in the oil industry at a 10% discount and earn a consistently high income, this fund offers a good alternative.

Please note that PEO changed its distribution policy in 2024 and committed to distributing at an 8% annualized rate (or 2% on a quarterly basis) of the fund's average net asset value ("NAV").The fees charged by the fund are very reasonable for a closed-end fund and are much lower compared to its peers at 0.64%. The fund uses no leverage.

Some top holdings of PEO are well-known names such as Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), ConocoPhillips (COP), Linde (LIN), EOG Resources (EOG), and Marathon Petroleum (MRO). It is a bit concentrated at the top, given the fact the two oil majors, XOM and CVX, together represent nearly 37% of the total assets.

  • Nuveen NASDAQ 100 Dynamic Overwrite Fund

QQQX is being repeated from last month. Every month, we usually keep one fund from the "covered call" category. These types of funds provide the best of both worlds: decent income and inflation-beating growth over a long period of time, while also lowering volatility. Other alternative funds in this category that figured in the top 20 or top 50 were Eaton Vance Risk-Managed Diversified Equity Income (ETJ), Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund (ETY), BlackRock Enhanced Large Cap Core Fund (CII), and Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund (STK).

QQQX holds some of the well-known and high-growth established names from the NASDAQ-100 index and writes option calls mostly on the indexes (Nasdaq-100 and S&P500). The performance record for the past 3-YR, 5-YR, and 10-YR has been pretty good at 10.30%, 11.98%, and 11.90%.

The top-10 names include Apple, Microsoft, NVIDIA, Amazon, Broadcom, Alphabet, Meta, and Tesla (TSLA).

  • KKR Income Opportunities Fund

Last month, we highlighted the fund Barings Global Short Duration High Yield (BGH). In many ways, KIO is similar to BGH in terms of the type of investments, leverage, and goals. They both fully cover their distributions with net investment income. KIO yields slightly higher than BGH, but their long-term performances (in terms of total returns) are pretty similar.

KIO mainly invests in leveraged loans and below-investment-grade high-yield securities. The focus on the short duration of the bonds keeps the fund's sensitivity to interest rates at a minimum. The yield on the basis of regular distribution is 11.45%. Its distribution has been very stable and consistent, though it reduced the distribution amount in the year 2020 and subsequently increased it in Jan. 2023. Its performance is decent, considering it is a bond fund, and beats its benchmark. That said, its 10-year total returns are less than what it yields, and that would be generally indicative of overpaying the distributions. However, it has also been a function of market conditions over the last few years. The fund also uses a significant amount of leverage, roughly 35%.

  • XAI Octagon FR & Alternative Inc Trust

In some ways, this fund is a retiree's dream fund in terms of income, but sure, there are some risks, as nothing comes for free. Moreover, the fund uses high leverage. This fund can be used as a means of diversification and elevating the income yield; however, it is best to avoid going overweight. This fund is somewhat complex to understand, but this is how the fund describes itself, "The Trust invests in a dynamically managed portfolio of floating-rate credit instruments and other structured credit investments within the private markets. Under normal market conditions, the Trust will invest at least 80% of managed assets in senior secured loans, CLO debt, and CLO equity." Below is the slide on investment allocation from the fund's website:

Chart-1:

As we can see, the fund is invested to the extent of 46% in Senior First-Lien Loans, which are considered relatively safer. However, it also invests nearly 50% of its assets in CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations) and nearly 3/4th of those in CLO equity tranches, which are considered the riskier part of CLOs. Its closest competitor fund in its space is Eagle Point Credit (ECC).

A fund like this can have relatively high volatility during times of crisis. For instance, during the peak of the pandemic in 2020, this fund had a drawdown of 40% compared to 24% for the S&P500. However, with the benefit of hindsight, that was also a good time to accumulate this fund.

The fund is actively managed, and managers are highly experienced in the CLO space. Even though it paid a special distribution in Dec. 2024, it also cut down the regular distribution amount by roughly 9%. That is also part of the reason that the fund's premium has disappeared entirely. Another reason for the downtrend is that fixed-income securities have sold off in recent months (and are possibly due for a rebound). Even then, this fund overpays the distribution if we look at NAV returns over many different time frames. That said, it yields over 14%, and one can easily reinvest 30% to 40% back into the fund to provide growth.

CEF-Specific Investment Risks

It goes without saying that CEFs generally have some additional risks. This section is specifically relevant for investors who are new to CEF investing, but in general, all CEF investors should be aware of it. For more details on these specific risks, please read our blog post on CEFs.

  • Use of high leverage and high fees
  • High volatility (compared to the broader market)
  • Premium over NAVs (market price higher than the NAV)
  • Asset-specific concentration risk.

Concluding Thoughts

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal yield of less than 1.25%. Income investors and retirees can't survive on that kind of income. For Income investors, we recommend building a core bucket of DGI (Dividend growth) stocks and supplementing it with a basket of CEFs to increase the overall income.

We use our screening process to highlight five likely best closed-end funds for investment each month. At the same time, we also provide a larger list of ten CEFs from many asset classes. Our filtering process demands that our selections have an above-average long-term performance record, offer an average of roughly 8% distributions, and are relatively cheaper with reasonable discounts.

This month, as a group, the selected funds are offering an average distribution rate of well over 10% (as of 02/14/2025). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had a decent record over the long term and collectively returned 11.26% and 9.93% in the last five and ten years, annualized. The 3-year average return is reasonable, too, at 8.6%. The average discount (to NAV) is decent, at nearly -6.8%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Please note that these selections are based on our proprietary rating system and are dynamic in nature. So, they can change from month to month (or even week to week). At the same time, some funds can repeat from one month to the next if they remain attractive over an extended period. Furthermore, note that if a fund has not made it to our list, it does not mean it is not good. It could be because they may not be attractively priced at this time or trading at a premium while running our filtering process.

.

data.title: Ariel Investments Focused Value Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Markets worldwide defied expectations in 2024 led by the dominating performance of mega-cap technology stocks known as the “magnificent seven. 1” Even though gains have been concentrated, the results of the U.S. election, strong earnings momentum and a robust labor market drove bullish sentiment. This ebullience extended beyond the equity market: gold delivered its best year since 2010, bitcoin more than doubled and the U.S. dollar neared a 20-year high. Meanwhile, uncertainty surrounding the new U.S. administration’s policies and the Federal Reserve signaling slower than anticipated easing in the year ahead drove small cap issues sharply lower in December. Although uncertainty and volatility are likely to remain elevated, the patient investor knows “stock prices trade on fundamentals. And when those solid fundamentals shine through, share prices rise. 2” Against this backdrop, the Ariel Focused Value Composite fell -0.76% gross of fees (-0.85% net of fees) in the quarter, ahead of the Russell 1000 Value Index’s -1.98% decline, but short of the S&P 500 Index’s +2.41% gain. Over the trailing one-year period, the Ariel Focused Value Composite advanced +14.35% (+13.95% net of fees), slightly behind the Russell 1000 Value which grew +14.37% and more significantly below the “magnificent seven” juiced S&P 500 Index, which returned +25.02%.

Tool innovator, Snap-on Incorporated (SNA) was the top contributor to performance in the period as the company continues to effectively navigate the growing complexity of the automotive repair industry. Amidst a challenging macro backdrop, SNA began redirecting product design, capacity and marketing efforts towards more affordable hand tools resulting in a sequential improvement in quarterly sales and gross margin expansion. We believe these results highlight SNA’s differentiated value proposition to its end markets, particularly as it continues to respond to real-time feedback and invest in new products to service unique needs of original equipment manufacturers. In our view, the automotive repair industry sports a favorable runway due to aging vehicles and the increased technological complexity associated with repair.

Global investment bank, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) also outperformed on a robust quarterly earnings beat, highlighted by strength across its investment banking, trading and asset management segments. Meanwhile, the U.S. election has been widely viewed as a positive catalyst across the industry. Investors expect the incoming administration to emphasize deregulation and exhibit a greater openness to business combinations compared to the prior regime. Hence, management’s positive commentary around the operating momentum of its core franchises, an improving M&A outlook and the resilience of the U.S. economy sent shares higher.

Additionally, supplier of residential thermal, comfort and security solutions, Resideo Technologies, Inc. (REZI) traded higher over the period. Despite a challenging macro environment, positive organic sales growth and structural cost efficiencies drove margin expansion. Encouraging commentary around improving consumer demand trends, new product offerings and cross-selling opportunities further aided share price appreciation. Also in the quarter, President and CEO, Jay Geldmacher, announced his intention to retire from his executive and board roles in 2025. Once a successor is identified, Mr. Geldmacher expects to become a senior advisor to the company to facilitate a smooth transition. In our view, this leadership change is indicative of the successful completion of the transformation plan the team initiated following the spin-out from Honeywell. We believe the company is entering a new phase of sustainable growth which will be driven by a secular preference for more connected smart home solutions and product innovation.

In contrast, shares of manufacturer and distributor of floorcovering products, Mohawk Industries Inc. (MHK) declined in the period as consumer demand and pricing remain under pressure due to secular headwinds in the housing market. Although quarterly earnings results were in-line with expectations, management lowered near-term guidance to account for losses resulting from recent U.S. hurricanes.

Meanwhile, management continues to successfully execute on productivity and cost restructuring initiatives, while also preparing the business for share gains as demand recovers. In our view, MHK’s healthy balance sheet and progress managing through economic cycles should help the company benefit from long-term growth in residential remodeling, new home construction and commercial projects.

Shares of leading global defense contractor Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) also traded lower in the period returning some of its third quarter gains. Although earnings were solid, investor uncertainty tied to ongoing F-35 contract negotiations and software delays overshadowed the company’s robust order backlog and return of capital to shareholders via share repurchases and dividends. In our view, LMT continues to be well positioned in the defense sector.

Lastly, gold mining company, Barrick Gold Corporation (GOLD) fell following an investor day where management reduced five-year guidance for gold production and raised cost estimates. Meanwhile, a dispute with the African government of Mali and associated negative headlines created an overhang on shares. Despite ongoing uncertainty, management remains laser focused on upgrading its mining operations and broadly improving efficiencies amid today’s rising prices for precious metals. The company also continues to prioritize capital returns to shareholders via dividends and share repurchases.

At current valuation levels, we believe the risk/reward is priced in.

Also during the quarter, we initiated a position in Schlumberger NV (SLB), the largest oilfield services company in the world by revenue. SLB provides equipment, services, and digital tools to help oil and gas producers operate more efficiently, including reservoir characterization, rig and well construction and production enhancement. We believe the company’s scale and technical expertise serves as a key differentiator. Weak near-term demand, an oil glut, falling commodity prices and concerns about future spending amid a global shift to renewable energies presented an attractive entry point. We believe there are tailwinds supporting rising demand over the medium-term, as national oil companies invest in long-cycle projects to grow capacity and address the natural decline of production. Additionally, we expect SLB will continue to evolve their capabilities to help clients with rising energy needs going forward.

By comparison, we successfully exited leading global manufacturer of power generation equipment, Generac Holdings, Inc. (GNRC) on valuation.

We believe the U.S. economy looks healthy and expect corporate profits to prove resilient. Although interest rates will likely settle at a higher structural level compared to the past decade and potential policies around tariffs and immigration present uncertainty, we think market friendly policies such as tax cuts and deregulation should support the broadening of the market beyond the Magnificent Seven. Once the performance gap between mega-cap stocks and smaller company counterparts narrows, our portfolios should be rewarded. We strongly believe the disciplined investor that stays the course and consistently owns differentiated businesses with solid competitive positioning and robust balance sheets will deliver superior returns over time.

Footnotes

1 The “Magnificent Seven” are the largest stocks in the S&P 500 Index driving market performance: Apple Inc. (AAPL), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Meta Platforms Inc. (META), Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA) and Tesla, Inc. (TSLA).

2 Hobson, Mellody and John W. Rogers Jr. “What the Stock market Taught Us This Year: Don’t Fall for These Investing Traps.” Wall Street Journal, 5 December 2023.

Investing in equity stocks is risky and subject to market volatility. A focused portfolio may be subject to greater volatility than a diversified portfolio. The intrinsic value of the stocks in which the portfolio invests may never be recognized by the broader market. The portfolio is often concentrated in fewer sectors than its benchmarks, and its performance may suffer if these sectors underperform the overall stock market.

Past performance does not guarantee future results. For the period ended 12/31/24, the performance (net of fees) for the Ariel Focused Value Composite for the 1-, 5-, and 10-year periods was +13.95%, +7.62%, and +6.75%, respectively. For the period ended 12/31/24 the performance for the Russell 1000 Value Index and the S&P 500 Index for the 1-, 5-, and 10-year periods was +14.37%, +8.68%, and +8.48%, and +25.02%, +14.52%, and +13.10%, respectively. Ariel Composite Net of Fees returns are calculated by deducting: (1) for the period from inception to December 31, 2013, the maximum advisory fee in effect for the respective period, applied on a monthly basis; and (2) for the period from January 1, 2014 onwards, the actual monthly advisory fee (on an asset-weighted basis) accrued for the accounts in the composite, using the fee rates in place as of the most recent calendar quarter-end. Gross returns do not reflect the deduction of advisory fees. Client returns will be reduced by advisory fees and such other expenses as may be incurred in the management of the account. Advisory fees are described in Part 2 of Ariel’s Form ADV. Returns assume the reinvestment of dividends and other earnings. Returns are expressed in U.S. dollars. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. The Ariel Focused Value Composite differs from its benchmark with dramatically fewer holdings concentrated in fewer sectors.

The opinions are current as of the date of this commentary but are subject to change. The information provided in this commentary does not provide information reasonably sufficient upon which to base an investment decision and should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any particular security. There is no guarantee that any of the views expressed will come to fruition or any investment will perform as described.

As of 12/31/24, Snap-on, Inc. constituted 6.7% of the Ariel Focused Value Composite (representative portfolio); Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. 5.8%; Resideo Technologies, Inc. 5.2%; Mohawk Industries, Inc. 4.8%; Lockheed Martin Corporation 4.3%; Barrick Gold Corporation 2.7%; Schlumberger Limited 0.5%; and Generac Holdings 0.0%. Portfolio holdings are subject to change. The performance of any single portfolio holding is no indication of the performance of other portfolio holdings of the Ariel Focused Value Composite.

A glossary of financial terms provided herein may be found on our website at www.arielinvestments.com.

Index returns reflect the reinvestment of income and other earnings. Indexes are unmanaged, and investors cannot invest directly in an index. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios, lower forecasted growth values and lower sales per share historical growth. Its inception date is January 1, 1987. Russell® is a trademark of London Stock Exchange Group, which is the source and owner of the Russell Indexes' trademarks, service marks and copyrights. Neither Russell nor its licensors accept any liability for any errors or omissions in the Russell Indexes or underlying data and no party may rely on any Russell Indexes and/or underlying data contained in this communication. No further distribution of Russell data is permitted without Russell's express written consent. Russell does not promote, sponsor or endorse the content of this communication. The S&P 500® Index is widely regarded as the best gauge of large-cap U.S. equities. It includes 500 leading companies and covers approximately 80% of available U.S. market capitalization. Its inception date is March 4, 1957.
